sound_type: accelerating wing-drum display on fallen log -- wings beat air while stationary, creating low-frequency pressure pulses that accelerate from 2/sec to 20/sec; fourth mechanical sound in the engine; E=5
ecosystem: Forest -- mixed deciduous-coniferous forest with dense understory. Young, regenerating forests with aspen, birch, alder, mixed conifers. Fallen logs for drumming. Most widely distributed grouse in North America.
season: Year-round resident. Drumming peaks April-June. Dawn and dusk. Hundreds of bouts per day during peak breeding.
family: Phasianidae (3rd entry. THIRD PHASIANIDAE. First mechanical Phasianidae entry.)

coordinates.description: Ruffed Grouse in PNW forests with alder, birch, cottonwood, and mixed conifers. Riparian corridors and regenerating areas. Both sides of Cascades where habitat conditions are met.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD Phasianidae entry. FOURTH MECHANICAL SOUND in engine. The most widespread grouse in North America. Drumming display is one of the most studied mechanical sounds in ornithology. Genus Bonasa: monotypic in North America.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Phasianidae: third entry. Wild Turkey (153) + Dusky Grouse (154) + Ruffed Grouse (155). Sooty Grouse (156) follows.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bonasa from Latin 'bonasus' (bison) -- referring to the drumming sound resembling a distant bison. umbellus from Latin 'umbrella, sunshade' -- referring to the ruff of neck feathers that the male fans during display.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bonasa -- first entry. Monotypic genus in North America. One species: Ruffed Grouse.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Galliformes. Third entry (after Wild Turkey 153, Dusky Grouse 154).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Third Phasianidae. First mechanical sound from the gamebird family. The wing-drum separates the Dusky-Sooty split pair (154, 156).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Individual beats: approximately 40-100 Hz. Peak acceleration rate: ~20 beats/sec = 20 Hz buzz at threshold of hearing.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 40-100 Hz. Low-frequency. Propagates effectively through forest understory.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Drumming beats: 40-100 Hz. Not a vocalization -- mechanical air compression.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Complete bout: 8-11 seconds. ~50 wing beats. Acceleration from 2/sec to 20/sec.
song_metrics.note_structure: WING-DRUM: Each beat produced by powerful forward-upward cupped wing stroke. Air compression/vacuum cycle creates low-frequency pressure pulse. No contact with body, log, or other wing.
song_metrics.note_types: Wing-drum (primary, breeding display -- mechanical), clucking (contact/alarm -- vocal), hissing (distress/aggression -- vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Acceleration pattern: slow (2/sec) > building > fast (20/sec) > blur > abrupt stop. Complete bout ~8-11 seconds. Repeated throughout dawn/dusk display sessions.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep, thumping, building to a low-frequency roar. 'A distant motor starting.' Mechanical -- not vocal. The sound of air being compressed by cupped wings.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Audible 200-400 meters. Low frequency aids forest propagation. Not extremely loud in dB but effectively far-carrying.
song_metrics.tempo: Acceleration: ~2 beats/sec (initial) to ~20 beats/sec (final). The acceleration is the defining characteristic.
song_metrics.context: Breeding season (April-June). Dawn and dusk. Male territorial/courtship display from drumming log. Hundreds of bouts per day.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minor variation in acceleration rate and bout length between populations. Consistent pattern across range.
song_metrics.key_property: WING-DRUM -- 40-100 Hz, accelerating 2-20 beats/sec, 8-11 seconds, stationary on log, wings beat air creating pressure pulses. E=5. THIRD PHASIANIDAE. FOURTH MECHANICAL SOUND. 'A distant motor starting.' Log selection critical. Air compression model.

plumage.overall: Two color morphs: gray and red-brown. Both are cryptically patterned with barring and mottling for forest floor camouflage. Males: black ruff of neck feathers (the 'umbrella' of umbellus), fanned during display. Black tail band.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Black tail band (diagnostic). Neck ruff (males). Cryptic mottling. Two color morphs (gray/brown). Small crest.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Males display ruff and fan tail during breeding season.
plumage.flight_pattern: Explosive burst flight when flushed. Loud wing whir. Short distance. Rapid acceleration. One of the most startling bird flushes.

physical_description.overall: Length: 40-50 cm. Weight: 450-750 g. Medium grouse. Smaller than Dusky/Sooty Grouse. Stocky build, short tail, small head.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Black tail band. Cryptic forest-floor coloring. Ruff of neck feathers. Two color morphs.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the drumming -- 'a distant motor starting.' Often heard long before seen. On the ground in dense forest. Explosive flush when disturbed.

nodes.title: Wing-Drum -- Air Compression Display
nodes.description: Wings beat air while bird stands stationary on log. Cupped wing creates localized vacuum followed by air rush, producing low-frequency pressure pulse. ~50 beats per bout. Tail braced against log for support. No contact with anything but air.

nodes.title: Acceleration Pattern -- 2 to 20 Beats/Second
nodes.description: Defining characteristic: acceleration from ~2 beats/sec to ~20 beats/sec over 8-11 seconds. Individual thumps merge into continuous low-frequency roar. 'A distant motor starting.' Abrupt stop.

nodes.title: Fourth Mechanical Sound in Engine
nodes.description: Goldeneye wing whistle (124, in-flight), Mourning Dove whistle (150, takeoff), Rock Pigeon clap (152, takeoff contact), Ruffed Grouse drum (155, stationary). Progression from locomotion byproduct to pure display.

nodes.title: Drumming Log Selection and Territory
nodes.description: Fallen log: 25-40 cm diameter, elevated, dense cover. Multi-year fidelity. Possible resonating platform (mass + air beneath). Territory center. The log defines the territory; the drumming marks the boundary.

cross_references.relationship: MECHANICAL PAIR! Rock Pigeon wing clap (152, wings strike each other) vs. Ruffed Grouse drum (155, wings beat air). Contact vs. compression. Adjacent mechanical sounds.
MECHANICAL BOOKENDS! First (124, in-flight whistle) and fourth (155, stationary drum) mechanical sounds. From locomotion to pure display.
GROUSE CONTRAST! Dusky booming (154, vocal, subsonic) vs. Ruffed drumming (155, mechanical, accelerating). Adjacent grouse, different mechanisms.
GROUSE TRIO! Dusky booming (154) > Ruffed drumming (155) > Sooty booming (156). Mechanical separates the split vocal pair.
